hook: The grand 1914 terminus at the head of the old Dakar-Niger railway, mothballed for a decade and reborn as the TER station.
facts: The present station building was erected 1913-1914 and entered service in June 1914, replacing an earlier 1885 station.
It stood at the Atlantic end of the Dakar-Niger railway, the colonial line running roughly 1,200 km inland to Bamako.
Passenger service collapsed and the building was shuttered around 2009 after the Dakar-Niger line ceased operating.

After a rehabilitation completed in 2018-2019 (with Aga Khan Trust for Culture involvement), it became the Dakar terminus of the Train Express Regional (TER), which entered commercial service on 27 December 2021.
The restored building is frequently cited as a masterpiece of Senegal's colonial-era architecture.
w: The grand Beaux-Arts terminus at the head of the old Dakar-Niger railway, the colonial line running 1,200 km inland to Bamako. Built in 1914, it fell silent around 2009 when the trains stopped, and stood shuttered for a decade. A restoration brought it back as the gleaming terminus of the new express line in 2021. A masterpiece, woken up.

hook: The Medina's landmark mosque, its 67-metre minaret modelled on Casablanca and inaugurated by a king of Morocco and a poet-president.
facts: Inaugurated on 27 March 1964 by King Hassan II of Morocco and Senegalese President Leopold Sedar Senghor.
Its minaret stands roughly 67 metres tall.
It was designed in a Moroccan / Arab-Andalusian idiom, modelled in part on the Mohammed V mosque in Casablanca, with Moroccan craftsmen contributing to the work.
It sits in the Medina on the Allees Pape Gueye Fall, the heart of Dakar's historic African quarter.
w: The Medina's landmark mosque, its 67-metre minaret modelled on Casablanca's -- inaugurated in 1964 by a king of Morocco and the poet-president Senghor side by side. Moroccan craftsmen worked its Arab-Andalusian detail. It rises over the Allees at the heart of Dakar's historic African quarter. Two nations' handshake, in stone and tile.

hook: A 1930s cathedral built as a memorial to Africa's WWI dead, fusing Sudanese towers and Byzantine domes with caryatids carved as Peul women.
facts: Built between 1923 and 1929 and consecrated on 2 February 1936; also known as Notre-Dame-des-Victoires, it is the seat of the Archbishop of Dakar.
It was conceived to commemorate the African soldiers who died fighting for France in the First World War, hence 'Souvenir Africain'.
It was raised on the site of a former Lebou cemetery on Boulevard de la Republique in the Plateau.
Designed by architect Charles-Albert Wulffleff, it blends Sudanese-style towers and Byzantine domes, with facade caryatids modelled on young Peul (Fulani) women.
Materials were drawn from across Africa, including pink sandstone, Tunisian marble and Gabonese hardwoods.
w: A cathedral raised in the 1920s as a memorial to the African soldiers who died for France in the Great War -- Sudanese towers, Byzantine domes, and facade caryatids carved as young Peul women. Wulffleff built it on a former Lebou cemetery, of pink sandstone, Tunisian marble and Gabonese hardwood. Empire's debt, paid in a fusion of styles.

n: IFAN Musee Theodore Monod d'Art africain
hook: A 1930s neo-Sudanese palace on Place Soweto holding one of West Africa's greatest collections of masks and African art.
facts: Housed in a neo-Sudanese / Art Deco building of 1931-1932 that had been the residence of the commander of the Dakar district, on Place Soweto near the National Assembly.
It is the museum of the Institut Fondamental d'Afrique Noire (IFAN), founded in 1936 under the French Popular Front government.
Its Dogon and Senufo mask collection, gathered on ethnographic missions from the 1940s-1960s, is among the rarest in the world.
In December 2007 it was officially renamed for the French naturalist Theodore Andre Monod, a former director of IFAN.
Its holdings run to thousands of objects, with hundreds on permanent display across the pavilions.
w: A neo-Sudanese palace of 1932 on Place Soweto, holding one of West Africa's greatest collections of masks. The research institute IFAN gathered its rare Dogon and Senufo pieces on ethnographic missions through the mid-century. Renamed in 2007 for the naturalist Theodore Monod, who once ran it. Thousands of faces, carved and watching.

hook: A vast 2018 pan-African museum shaped like a Casamance impluvium hut, realising a dream Senghor first voiced in 1966.
facts: Inaugurated on 6 December 2018 by President Macky Sall, opposite the Grand Theatre National in the Plateau.
The idea dated to Senegal's first president Leopold Sedar Senghor and the first World Festival of Negro Arts (Dakar, 1966).
The circular building is inspired by the traditional impluvium huts of the Casamance region, with roughly 14,000 square metres of floor space over four levels.
Construction took about seven years and over 30 million euros, with the building financed by China.
Its mission spans the cultural heritage of Africa and of the global African diaspora, with capacity for many thousands of exhibits.
w: A vast pan-African museum shaped like a round Casamance impluvium hut, opened in 2018 -- realising a dream Senghor first voiced at the 1966 festival of Black arts. Four levels and 14,000 square metres tell the story of Africa and its diaspora. China paid to build it. Half a century from idea to doors. A continent, given a hall of its own.

hook: A round wrought-iron colonial market hall of 1860 that burned to the ground in 1993 and was rebuilt, identically, with European money.
facts: The circular market building, of brick, wood and wrought iron, dates to 1860 in the Plateau quarter.
It has a cast-iron frame, brick walls and three large horseshoe-shaped entrances, with sections for foodstuffs and crafts.
It was gutted by a fire in September 1993.
Reconstruction, financed by Luxembourg and the European Union at a cost of roughly US$5 million, rebuilt it to the original design over about three years.
w: A round colonial market hall of 1860, all brick and wrought iron, with three great horseshoe doorways into the bustle of stalls. Fire gutted it in 1993; Luxembourg and the EU paid to rebuild it, identically, over three years. Flowers, fish and fruit under the old iron frame again. A Victorian rotunda that refused to stay burned.

hook: For ninety years Dakar's great Sudanese-style market hall stood here, until it was bulldozed in 2021 amid an architects' outcry.
facts: The landmark market hall was built in the Sudanese-Sahelian tradition in the 1930s (c. 1933-1935), its earthen-mosque geometry echoing the Sahel.
It stood at the corner of Avenue Lamine Gueye and Avenue Emile Badiane, between the colonial Plateau and working-class quarters.
Weakened by repeated fires and deemed at risk of collapse, the building was demolished by the authorities in 2021.
The demolition outraged Senegalese architects, who feared a modern replica could not preserve its heritage character.
w: For ninety years a great Sudanese-style market hall stood here -- its earthen-mosque geometry borrowed from the Sahel, a 1930s landmark at the edge of the Plateau. Weakened by repeated fires and called unsafe, it was bulldozed in 2021, to the fury of Senegal's architects, who doubted any replica could hold its soul.

hook: Dakar's African quarter, laid out in 1914 when a plague panic gave the colonial state its pretext to expel Africans from the Plateau.
facts: The Medina was established in 1914 to house the African population evicted from central Dakar.
Its creation followed a bubonic-plague epidemic that year, which the French administration used to justify racial segregation of the city.
Lebou and other African communities resisted the forced relocation through strikes, demonstrations and refusals of vaccination.
Through the 20th century the Medina grew into a dense, creatively charged quarter, set in deliberate contrast to the downtown Plateau.
legend: The 1914 plague was widely framed by colonial authorities as a sanitary necessity; historians instead read it as a constructed pretext for segregating Africans from Europeans.
w: Dakar's African quarter, laid out in 1914 when a bubonic-plague panic handed the colonial state its pretext to push Africans out of the Plateau. The Lebou fought the eviction with strikes and refusals of vaccination. Out of that segregation grew a dense, creative quarter, set against the downtown it was exiled from.

hook: A 49-metre bronze family striding off a volcanic hill — taller than the Statue of Liberty, conceived by a president and built by North Koreans.
facts: Unveiled 3-4 April 2010 to mark the 50th anniversary of Senegalese independence.
Roughly 49-52 m tall in bronze, making it taller than the Statue of Liberty's figure; it depicts a man, woman and child rising toward the sky and sea.
Built by Mansudae Overseas Projects, the North Korean state monument-construction company.
Concept by President Abdoulaye Wade and designed by Senegalese architect Pierre Goudiaby Atepa; reported cost about US$27 million.
Local imams condemned it as idolatrous over its semi-nude figures, and Wade's claim to 35% of tourism profits drew protest.
Houses an interior museum and an observation deck in the man's head, reached by lift.
w: A 49-metre bronze family striding off a volcanic hill -- a man, woman and child rising toward the sea, taller than the Statue of Liberty. President Wade conceived it for independence's 50th year in 2010; North Korea's Mansudae company cast it. Imams called the half-nude figures idolatrous; the cost drew protest.

hook: An 1864 lighthouse perched on the taller of Dakar's two volcanic 'breast' hills, its Fresnel lens still floating in mercury 120 m above the Atlantic.
facts: Completed in 1864 on the higher of the Deux Mamelles hills near Cap-Vert, the westernmost tip of Africa.
The tower is only about 16 m tall but has a focal height of roughly 120 m, giving a range near 31 nautical miles.
Uses a second-order Fresnel lens (Barbier, Benard et Turenne) that floats in mercury and flashes white every five seconds.
Power was upgraded in 1911 and again in 1954; it remains staffed rather than fully automated, managed by the Dakar port authority.
'Mamelles' (French for breasts) refers to the pair of volcanic hills that overhang Dakar.
w: An 1864 lighthouse on the taller of Dakar's two volcanic 'breast' hills, its beam 120 metres above the Atlantic and reaching 30 miles out. The tower is squat, but its second-order Fresnel lens floats in mercury and flashes white every five seconds. Still staffed, not automated, on the westernmost cape of Africa.

a: Ouakam, in the cove below the cliffs
hook: A white twin-minaret mosque wedged into an Ouakam cove at the water's edge, said to have been revealed in a dream and built almost entirely by hand.
facts: Stands in an arc-shaped cove on the Corniche-Ouest shore of Ouakam, its forecourt practically on the beach.
Designed by architect Cheikh Ngom; the first call to prayer sounded in 1997 after the foundation stone was laid in 1992.
Its twin minarets stand about 45 m tall.
Accounts say it was built almost without machinery, the community doing the work by hand after the site's only machine broke down within a week.
legend: By tradition the project began in 1973 when a holy man, Mohamed Gorgui Seyni Guèye, dreamed of a mosque and a voice told him to follow it; he is said to have followed the vision down to Ouakam Bay, where it 'descended from the sky' and settled like a sheet of paper at the spot where the mosque now stands.
w: A white twin-minaret mosque wedged into an Ouakam cove, its forecourt almost on the sand. The story goes a holy man dreamed of it in 1973 and followed a vision down to this bay, where the mosque 'settled like a sheet of paper'. The first call to prayer sounded in 1997; the community built it by hand after the one machine broke.

a: Almadies — the western tip of the Cap-Vert peninsula
hook: The westernmost point of mainland Africa and of the whole Afro-Eurasian landmass, where fishermen grill the morning's catch in shacks above the rocks.
facts: Lies on the north-western end of the Cap-Vert peninsula, the westernmost point of continental Africa and of the Afro-Eurasian landmass.
Sits at about 17.5 degrees west of the Greenwich meridian — roughly 8 degrees further west than Cabo da Roca, mainland Europe's westernmost point.
The point is rimmed by lava rock and informal seafood shacks where catch, including oysters, is grilled to order.
Forms part of the Cap-Vert volcanic peninsula that gives Dakar its dramatic basalt coastline.
w: The westernmost point of mainland Africa -- and of the whole Afro-Eurasian landmass, some eight degrees further west than Europe's edge. Lava rock runs down to the sea, lined with informal seafood shacks where the morning's oysters and fish are grilled to order. Land's end of a continent, with a plate in your hand.

a: Off Ngor village, reached by pirogue
hook: A car-free islet 400 m offshore, reached by painted pirogue, whose right-hand reef break made Dakar the opening scene of surfing's most famous film.
facts: A small island roughly 500 m by 200 m lying about 400 m north of the Pointe des Almadies, off-limits to motor vehicles.
Reached by a short crossing in traditional pirogues; beaches ring all sides of the island.
Its south-side reef break, 'Ngor Right' (also called Endless Summer), is a fast, hollow right over shallow lava rock.
In 1964 surfers Mike Hynson and Robert August rode this break for the opening sequence of Bruce Brown's film 'The Endless Summer', helping launch surfing in Senegal.
Ngor is one of the original Lebou 'tànk' villages of the peninsula, alongside Yoff and Ouakam.
w: A car-free islet 400 metres offshore, reached by painted pirogue from Ngor village -- beaches all around, no engines anywhere. Its south-side reef break, Ngor Right, is fast and hollow over shallow lava; in 1964 two surfers rode it for the opening scene of 'The Endless Summer', and Senegalese surfing took off.

a: Yoff — Layène quarter, on the beach
hook: A seaside tomb with sand floors in the fishing village of Yoff, holding the founder of the Layène Sufi order who declared himself the Mahdi on this coast.
facts: Holds the tomb of Seydina Mouhammadou Limamou Laye (1843-1909), founder of the Layène Sufi brotherhood, who was born in Yoff.
On 24 May 1884 he proclaimed himself the Mahdi, a declaration that drew controversy with French authorities and orthodox Muslims.
The mausoleum sits directly on the Yoff beach, facing the sea, with floors of sand.
It is a major pilgrimage site, especially for the annual 'Appel' ('The Call') commemorating his first public message in 1884.
The Layène teaching stressed cleanliness, prayer, alms and social justice; the order is smaller than the Mouride and Tijaniyya but distinctive.
legend: Layène devotion holds that Limamou Laye was the returned Prophet Muhammad and his son Seydina Issa a return of Jesus — beliefs that mark the brotherhood out from Senegal's other Sufi orders.
w: A sand-floored tomb on the Yoff beach, facing the sea, holding the founder of the Layene Sufi order. Born here in 1843, Limamou Laye proclaimed himself the Mahdi on this coast in 1884 -- to the alarm of the French and orthodox Islam alike. His followers hold him to be the Prophet returned. Pilgrims still gather for the Appel, the Call.

n: Plage et village de pêcheurs d'Ouakam
a: Ouakam — at the foot of the Mamelles cliffs
hook: A working Lebou fishing beach under the Mamelles cliffs, where pirogues land beneath the Renaissance Monument and the surf rolls into Secret Spot.
facts: Ouakam is one of the original Lebou villages of the Cap-Vert peninsula (with Yoff, Ngor and Hann), set at the foot of the Deux Mamelles hills.
Its beaches remain a working fishing ground, with colourful pirogues, net-mending and fish-sorting along the shore.
One of its beaches is a noted surf break; the nearby Almadies break 'Secret Spot' is where Senegal's first local surfers established themselves.
Urbanisation of Lebou land began with French colonial rule from the mid-1850s, yet Ouakam keeps Lebou ceremonies and community life today.
legend: Oral tradition credits the founding of Ouakam to a figure named Alé Ndoye, said to have chosen the site for its closeness to the sea.
w: A working Lebou fishing beach under the Mamelles cliffs, where pirogues land in the shadow of the Renaissance Monument and the surf rolls into a break called Secret Spot. Ouakam is one of the old Lebou villages of the cape; colonial Dakar grew up around it, yet its ceremonies and net-mending carry on.

hook: A coral-pink slave house whose narrow seaward doorway became the world's emblem of the Middle Passage — the 'Door of No Return'.
facts: Built around 1776 by Nicolas Pépin, brother of the signare Anne Pépin; it is the last surviving slave house of Gorée's many.
Opened as a museum in 1962 and curated for decades by Boubacar Joseph Ndiaye until his death in 2009.
Its rear 'Porte du Voyage sans Retour' (Door of No Return) opens directly onto the Atlantic and is the building's most famous feature.
Part of the Island of Gorée, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978 (one of the first twelve sites ever listed).
The painted coral-pink upper storey housed the slave traders while the enslaved were held in cramped ground-floor cells.
legend: It is widely told that millions of captives passed through this single door; historians (notably the debate around Ndiaye's figures) argue the actual numbers shipped from this specific house were far smaller, and the site is best understood as a symbolic memorial rather than a literal mass-export point.
w: A coral-pink house on Goree whose narrow seaward doorway became the world's emblem of the Middle Passage -- the Door of No Return. Built around 1776, the last of the island's slave houses, it opened as a museum in 1962. Historians debate the actual numbers shipped from this one door; its power is as a memorial, not a tally.

hook: Gorée's basalt high point, crowned with rusting coastal guns and a sail-shaped slave memorial, looking back across the bay to Dakar.
facts: A roughly 34-metre basalt plateau at the island's southern tip, the highest ground on Gorée and long its key defensive position.
In 1935 two 240 mm guns from the scrapped battleship Vergniaud were hoisted to the summit; gun emplacements, cisterns and walls survive.
A modern Slave Memorial shaped like a ship's hull and sail was inaugurated here in 1999.
The plateau incorporates the old Fort Saint-Michel area and gives a panorama over Dakar harbour and the open Atlantic.
w: Goree's basalt high point, crowned with the rusting guns of a scrapped French battleship and a 1999 slave memorial. The 34-metre plateau held the island's key fort for centuries; cisterns and emplacements still ring it. From the top, Dakar's towers float across the bay. Cannon, memory and a long view back to the mainland.

hook: A horseshoe-shaped seafront citadel on Gorée's north tip, now IFAN's national history museum from prehistory to independence.
facts: Built by the French between 1852 and 1856 as a coastal fortress guarding the northern approach to the island.
Assigned to IFAN (Institut Fondamental d'Afrique Noire) in 1977; after restoration it reopened as the Musée historique du Sénégal on 3 March 1989.
Its galleries trace Senegal's history from origins to independence, with a focus on Gorée and the Atlantic slave trade.
Sits at the island's northern point, distinct from the Castel fortifications at the southern tip.
w: A horseshoe-shaped seafront citadel on Goree's north tip, built by the French in the 1850s to guard the island's approach. IFAN took it over and reopened it in 1989 as Senegal's national history museum,. A fortress that now tells the story it once enforced.

hook: One of Senegal's oldest churches, rebuilt by Gorée's signares on the ashes of a chapel the English torched on Christmas Eve 1799.
facts: First stone laid 9 November 1828; the church was completed in June 1830 on rue du Chevalier de Boufflers at the island's centre.
It replaced an earlier church burned by English soldiers during the night of Christmas 1799 when they retook the island.
Construction was financed by the signares, the wealthy Afro-European merchant women of Gorée.
The building measures about 30 by 15 metres and its bell weighs roughly 115 kg; it lies within the UNESCO World Heritage zone.
It celebrates its patronal feast of Saint Charles Borromeo around 4 November each year.
w: One of Senegal's oldest churches, raised on Goree between 1828 and 1830 on the ashes of a chapel English soldiers torched on Christmas night, 1799. The signares -- the wealthy Afro-European merchant women of the island -- paid to rebuild it. Its bell weighs some 115 kilos; it stands in the UNESCO-listed heart of the island.

hook: A small whitewashed mosque of black basalt stone tucked at the foot of the Castel cliff — among the oldest stone mosques in Senegal.
facts: Built in 1890 from Senegalese basaltic stone, it stands at the foot of the western slope of the Castel.
It is counted among the oldest surviving stone mosques in Senegal.
The single minaret faces the ocean; the modest white building sits within Gorée's UNESCO-listed historic fabric, a short walk from the island's churches.
w: A small whitewashed mosque of black basalt, tucked at the foot of the Castel cliff -- among Senegal's oldest stone mosques, built in 1890. Its single minaret faces the ocean. A short walk separates it from the island's churches, Muslim and Christian Goree sharing the same few cobbled lanes. The quiet counterpart to the famous slave house.

hook: A strawberry-pink salt lake saltier than the sea, worked by waist-deep harvesters — and for two decades the finish line of the Paris–Dakar Rally.
facts: Lies about 35 km north-east of Dakar; covers roughly 3 km² with a maximum depth of around 3 m.
Its pink hue comes from the salt-loving micro-alga Dunaliella salina, most vivid in the dry season (November–May).
Salinity reaches up to about 40% in places, rivalling the Dead Sea, so workers float and rub on shea butter to protect their skin.
Harvesters extract on the order of tens of thousands of tonnes of salt a year by hand from the lakebed.
It was frequently the finishing point of the Dakar Rally before the event relocated to South America in 2009.
It has been on Senegal's UNESCO World Heritage tentative list since 2005.
w: A strawberry-pink salt lake saltier than the sea, its colour from a salt-loving alga that blooms in the dry season. Harvesters wade in waist-deep, slicked in shea butter against the brine, and scoop tens of thousands of tonnes of salt by hand. For two decades the Paris-Dakar Rally crossed its finish line on this shore.

a: Îles de la Madeleine, off Soumbédioune
hook: A wind-stunted volcanic islet of dwarf baobabs and tropicbird colonies — billed as the smallest national park in the world, reached by pirogue from Soumbédioune.
facts: The national park covers just 0.45 km² (about 45 ha), often described as the smallest national park in the world.
Protected as an ornithological reserve from 1949 and made a national park by decree in 1976.
The basalt islands carry a population of wind-dwarfed baobabs; the main island Sarpan also yields Stone Age tool finds.
Breeding seabirds include red-billed tropicbird, white-breasted cormorant and bridled tern.
Lies roughly 4 km off Dakar, with boats departing the fishing port of Soumbédioune on the western Corniche.
legend: Local tradition speaks of seven sacred baobabs used as places of worship on the island, of which only the most important is approached; an 18th-century account by naturalist Michel Adanson of a giant inscribed baobab here could not be confirmed when Théodore Monod later searched for it.
w: A volcanic islet of wind-stunted dwarf baobabs and nesting tropicbirds, four kilometres off the Corniche -- billed as the smallest national park on earth, 45 hectares in all. Pirogues cross from the Soumbedioune fish port. Local tradition keeps seven sacred baobabs here, only the chief one approached. A wild speck of basalt and bird.

n: Parc forestier et zoologique de Hann
hook: A century-old colonial botanical garden and zoo on Hann Bay, a green lung of arboretum, classified forest and crocodile pools inside the city.
facts: Created in 1903 by French colonial administrator Martial Henri Merlin as a garden and plant nursery.
Opened to the public around 1935; an arboretum to conserve West African plants was added in 1947.
Today it spreads over roughly 60 hectares combining classified forest, arboretum, botanical garden, nursery and a zoo.
It sits in the Hann Bel-Air commune on Hann Bay, a few kilometres east of central Dakar.
Animal collections have included lions, primates, crocodiles and birds, though the zoo has faced long-running calls for renovation.
w: A century-old botanical garden and zoo on Hann Bay -- 60 hectares of arboretum, classified forest and crocodile pools breathing green inside the city. A French administrator planted it as a nursery in 1903; an arboretum to save West African plants followed in 1947. The zoo has long needed care, but the trees endure.

hook: A faded colonial peanut port east of Dakar — one of Senegal's 'Four Communes' whose Africans won full French citizenship a century before independence.
facts: Founded by Lebou people and built up by French merchants as a major peanut export and trading town.
One of the Quatre Communes (Four Communes) with Saint-Louis, Dakar and Gorée whose inhabitants gained rights of French citizenship from 1848.
The Dakar–Saint-Louis railway reached Rufisque in 1885, driving its commercial boom; the old station and platform survive.
Lies about 25 km east of Dakar at the base of the Cap-Vert peninsula, often called the 'gateway to Dakar'.
Many examples of its grand colonial-era architecture still stand, if weathered.
w: A faded peanut port east of Dakar, once one of Senegal's Four Communes -- whose Africans won full French citizenship from 1848, a century before independence. The railway from Saint-Louis reached it in 1885 and lit a trading boom; weathered colonial grandeur still lines its streets. The 'gateway to Dakar', long past its prime.
